text: The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to separate the posterior peritoneum above the ruviate sulcus, ensuring the Teflon jaw is positioned towards the cystic pedicle to prevent vibration transfer to the gallbladder. The fundus is pushed towards the right shoulder, providing entromedial traction via the harassment pouch.

The surgeon applies anterolateral traction to open the anterior peritoneum above the roviate sulcus at the junction of the gallbladder body with the cystic pedicle, preparing to skeletonize the cystic pedicle.

The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to enlarge the posterior window by opening its jaws, then creates an anterior window between the cystic duct and artery. Three clips are applied: two on the right hepatic artery and one on the gallbladder side. The artery is then cut between the second and third clip.

The surgeon visualizes both limbs of the cystic duct, preparing to clip or ligate it. The duct is clipped by pressing halfway to form a ring, adjusting, and then fully pressing for three seconds. The clips are placed with a three-millimeter distance between the first and second, and a six-millimeter distance between the second and third.

The surgeon cuts the cystic duct and proceeds to separate the gallbladder from the liver using entromedial and entrolateral traction. The peritoneum of the gallbladder is carefully separated to achieve a clear tissue plane.

The surgeon adjusts the light cable of the laparoscopic telescope to enhance visibility of the angular plane, facilitating the separation of the gallbladder from the liver bed. Care is taken to avoid puncturing the gallbladder while using the harmonic device at the proper angular plane. As the last pedicle is separated, the surgeon checks below for any active bleeding or bile leak, noting that minor oozing is acceptable.
